1: 'Then we turned and journeyed into the wilderness in the direction of the Red Sea, and we went around Mount Seir many days' — the wilderness circuit of Seir (the Edomite hill country in modern southern Jordan) during the years of the condemned generation's dying. The Seir highlands rise steeply above the Arabah valley; the circuit around them rather than through them reflects the divine prohibition against provoking Edom. 'Many days' collapses years of wandering into a single verse — the narrative focus is on the obedient patience of the circuit, not its duration.
2: The divine speech break — 'then YHWH said to me' — marks the transition from passive waiting to active advance. YHWH's episodic direct speech to Moses throughout Deuteronomy 1-3 punctuates the historical retrospective with the divine authorizations that drove each stage. This is the suzerain-vassal communication model: the great king issues specific directives at each new stage of the vassal's assignment rather than a single comprehensive mandate to be executed independently.
3: 'You have been going around this mountain long enough. Turn north' — the termination order for the Seir circuit and the pivot toward the Transjordanian approach. The years of wilderness wandering ended not by Israel's initiative or by the natural exhaustion of their wandering but by YHWH's specific word. The directness of the command — no condition, no negotiation — reflects the suzerain's authority to determine the vassal's movement schedule. The turn northward begins the purposive phase of the Transjordanian campaign.
4: 'You are about to pass through the territory of your brothers, the Esau people who live in Seir. They will be afraid of you' — 'brothers' (ʾaḥêkem) as the covenant-kinship term for Edom grounds the relationship in the Jacob-Esau fraternal bond (Genesis 25:19-34; 27; 32-33). Edomite fear of Israel's military capacity is acknowledged and must be managed with non-aggressive behavior signals. The diplomatic protocol reflects the real political sensitivity of passing a hostile kinship-people's territorial boundary.
5: 'I will not give you any of their land — not even enough to set a foot on — because I have given Mount Seir to Esau as a possession' — the divine land-grant principle applied beyond Israel: YHWH's sovereignty over all nations' territories means every people occupies land as his grant, not by natural right. This passage is remarkable: YHWH explicitly restrains Israel's territorial ambition by appealing to his own prior gift to Esau's descendants. The covenant with Israel does not nullify YHWH's prior provision for other peoples.
6: 'You shall buy food from them with money so you may eat, and you shall also buy water from them with money so you may drink' — commercial exchange rather than military requisition as the transit protocol. The requirement to pay for provisions reflected the recognized diplomatic etiquette for caravan passage through sovereign territory. The Edomite route was an established trade corridor; commercial engagement rather than forced levy was the expected mode of peaceful transit through an autonomous state.
7: 'YHWH your God has blessed you in all the work of your hands' — the wilderness blessing (manna, water, preserved clothing and sandals: Deuteronomy 8:3-4) is cited as the reason Israel does not need to take by force what can be purchased. The forty years of divine provision demonstrated that YHWH meets Israel's material needs outside normal agricultural and commercial channels; purchasing from Edom extends this provision into conventional commerce rather than supernatural supply.
8: 'We passed on from our brothers, the sons of Esau who live in Seir, away from the Arabah road, from Elath and from Ezion-geber' — Elath (modern Eilat) and Ezion-geber (modern Tell el-Kheleifeh, near Aqaba) were the twin port cities at the head of the Gulf of Aqaba. These ports became central to Solomon's Red Sea trade fleet (1 Kings 9:26; Deuteronomy 10:11,22). Their casual mention reflects the narrator's post-Solomonic knowledge of the sites' significance; for the wilderness generation they were simply waypoints on the desert route.
9: 'Do not harass Moab, for I will not give you any of their land as a possession, because I have given Ar to the sons of Lot' — Moab and Ammon are identified as 'sons of Lot' (Genesis 19:36-38), born of Lot's daughters after Sodom's destruction. The Genesis narrative presents the circumstance as shameful; nevertheless YHWH grants their descendants legitimate territorial rights that Israel must respect. The moral complexity of Lot's daughters' deception is not resolved by withholding territorial rights from their descendants.
10: 'The Emim formerly lived there, a great and numerous and tall people, like the Anakim' — the ethnographic notes on pre-Moabite inhabitants reflect historical traditions about population displacement in the Transjordan before Israel's arrival. The Emim ('terrifying ones') are associated with Moab's territory. These notes serve a legitimating function: even the nations Israel must respect (Edom, Moab, Ammon) are themselves occupying land from which they displaced an earlier population — the pattern of YHWH-supervised population succession is universal.
11: 'Like the Anakim, these also are counted as Rephaim, but the Moabites call them Emim' — the Rephaim are the standard OT term for the pre-Israelite giant peoples of Canaan and Transjordan (Genesis 14:5; Deuteronomy 15:20; Deuteronomy 3:11,13). The term appears also in Ugaritic texts as a category of deified dead warriors or royal ancestors. Different local populations use different names (Emim in Moab, Zamzummim in Ammon, Anakim in Hebron) for what the Israelite tradition classifies under the single term Rephaim.
12: 'The Horites formerly lived in Seir, but the sons of Esau dispossessed them' — the Horites are connected by some scholars to the Hurrians, a significant Mesopotamian-origin people attested in the Nuzi texts and Egyptian records as present in Canaan during the Middle Bronze Age. The comparison 'as Israel did to the land of their possession which YHWH gave them' explicitly parallels Edom's displacement of the Horites with Israel's upcoming displacement of the Canaanites — establishing a cross-cultural precedent for YHWH-authorized territorial succession.
13: 'Now rise up and cross the Brook Zered' — the Wadi Zered (modern Wadi el-Hesa in southern Jordan) was the natural boundary between Edom and Moab, and its crossing marks Israel's entry into Moabite territory from the south. Major wadis in the Transjordanian plateau served as natural political boundaries; the Brook Zered's crossing was a recognized territorial landmark in the regional geography that the narrator treats as commonly known.
14: 'The time from our leaving Kadesh-barnea until we crossed the Zered brook was thirty-eight years, until the entire generation of warriors had perished from the camp' — the precise calculation: forty years total (Numbers 14:33-34) minus two years for the journey from Egypt to Kadesh = thirty-eight years at the Zered crossing. The accounting reflects careful covenantal precision: YHWH's oath was literally fulfilled — the exact generation that rejected the land died before crossing to the next territory. The years serve as the waiting period until the condemned cohort expires.
15: 'The hand of YHWH was against them, to destroy them from the camp, until they had perished' — divine agency in what appeared to be ordinary mortality. The deaths of the wilderness generation are not presented as miraculous intervention but as YHWH's providential direction of natural processes toward the covenant-sworn outcome. In the ANE worldview, there was no category of event that fell outside divine sovereignty; ordinary aging and death were no less expressions of YHWH's will than spectacular miracles.
16: The completion of the transition: 'all the men of war had perished and died from among the people.' The new generation ready to cross the Jordan is the wilderness-born cohort that knows only YHWH's covenantal provision — manna, water from the rock, preserved clothing — and has no living memory of Egypt's food or Egypt's servitude. The generational change is theologically as much as biologically significant: a new people without Egypt's psychological formation.
17: The renewed divine speech after the old generation's death — a covenant reset. The double-speech structure within chapter 2 (Deuteronomy 2:2 initiating the Seir circuit, Deuteronomy 2:17 initiating the Arnon advance) models YHWH's leadership as responsive and stage-by-stage: each new phase of the mission requires fresh authorization rather than being governed by a single standing order. The communication pattern prepares for the Joshua era when YHWH will speak directly to the new leader.
18: 'Today you are to cross the boundary of Moab at Ar' — the specific crossing point named ('at Ar') rather than a general permission to enter. Ar was the principal city of Moab (Numbers 21:28; Isaiah 15:1), located near the modern Wadi Mujib. The precision of the boundary crossing instruction reflects detailed knowledge of the Transjordanian road network and the political geography of Moabite territory. Israel's transit is tightly directed — not freedom to roam but a specified crossing point.
19: 'When you approach the territory of the sons of Ammon, do not harass them, for I will not give you the land of the sons of Ammon as a possession, because I have given it to the sons of Lot as a possession' — the same divine-grant formula applied to Ammon as to Moab. The consistent application reinforces the universal principle: YHWH's territorial assignments are categorical, not negotiable through military strength. Ammon occupied the Jabbok river highlands (the modern Zarqa basin in northern Jordan), a distinct territory from Moab's Arnon plateau.
20: 'That also is counted as a land of Rephaim; Rephaim formerly lived there, but the Ammonites call them Zamzummim' — Ammon's local name for the Rephaim inhabitants they displaced. The three-fold pattern (Horites in Seir/Edom; Emim in Moab; Zamzummim in Ammon) creates a systematic ethnographic framework: every nation currently in the Transjordan displaced an earlier, more impressive population. Israel's upcoming campaign against the Canaanites fits this pattern as the fourth and final instance of YHWH-supervised population succession.
21: 'A great and numerous and tall people, like the Anakim; but YHWH destroyed them before the Ammonites, and they dispossessed them and settled in their place' — YHWH is explicitly credited for the Zamzummim's destruction before Ammon, just as he will destroy the Canaanites before Israel. The theological principle is stated with stark clarity: the nations currently occupying territories hold them because YHWH cleared the prior inhabitants on their behalf. No nation's territorial tenure is based on its own intrinsic right; all land-holding is gift.
22: 'As he did for the sons of Esau who live in Seir, when he destroyed the Horites before them' — the 'to this day' formula (ʿad hayyôm hazzeh) confirms the ongoing visible reality of Edom occupying Seir: the narrator writes from a time when Edom's Seir-tenure was an observable fact. This historiographic device grounds the theological principle in observable history — not abstract claim but visible, verifiable outcome. The ANE historical consciousness that grounded current political arrangements in divine acts of the past is the frame for this note.
23: 'The Avvim who lived in villages as far as Gaza — the Caphtorim who came from Caphtor destroyed them and settled in their place' — Caphtor is widely identified with Crete or the broader Aegean world; the Caphtorim/Philistines connection is explicit in Amos 9:7 ('did I not bring Israel from Egypt, and the Philistines from Caphtor?'). The Sea Peoples' migration into the Levantine coast ca. 1200 BCE, including the Philistines who displaced the coastal Avvim, is the ANE historical event that this note preserves. The pattern extends to the Mediterranean coast: even the Philistine territory was held by prior occupants whom YHWH permitted the Caphtorim to displace.
24: 'Cross the valley of the Arnon. See, I have given into your hand Sihon the Amorite, king of Heshbon, and his land. Begin to take possession and contend with him in battle' — the holy war initiation formula at the Arnon crossing. The Arnon (modern Wadi Mujib, a spectacular 1,300-meter-deep canyon cutting through the Moabite plateau) was the recognized northern boundary of Moabite territory. Crossing it into Amorite territory initiates the offensive phase. The divine prophetic perfect 'I have given' treats the victory as already accomplished — the ANE oracle of divine victory that precedes the warrior-god's campaign.
25: 'I will begin to put the dread and fear of you on the peoples under the whole heaven, who shall hear the report of you and shall tremble and be in anguish' — the holy war fear-formula (cf. Exodus 23:27; Joshua 2:9-11). The function of divine terror preceding the army is attested in ANE royal inscriptions where the patron god's melammû (terrifying radiance) precedes the king into battle. Rahab's testimony in Joshua 2:9-11 confirms that this formula described a real psychological effect: the report of the Reed Sea and Sihon's defeat had already produced dread in Canaan before Israel crossed the Jordan.
26: 'So I sent messengers from the wilderness of Kedemoth to Sihon king of Heshbon, with words of peace' — dibrê šālôm (words of peace): the formal diplomatic peace-overture before military action. Deuteronomy 20:10-11 later codifies this as law: before sieging a city, offer terms of peace. Moses's diplomatic message to Sihon follows the protocol that will become statute — peaceful-passage request, commercial terms, commitment to stay on the road. The recognized right of peaceful transit through sovereign territory was a standard element in ANE international relations.
27: 'Let me pass through your land. I will travel only along the road; I will turn neither to the right nor to the left' — the transit-right formula for commercial caravan passage. The commitment to stay strictly on the designated road was the standard assurance against transit becoming occupation. ANE diplomatic correspondence (Amarna letters and later Assyrian correspondence) provides parallels for this exact formulation. The road (derek hamelek, the king's highway of Numbers 20:17) was the public right-of-way that custom recognized as available for peaceful transit.
28: 'You shall sell me food for money so that I may eat, and give me water for money so that I may drink' — the commercially equitable terms: Israel offers to pay market prices for provisions, claiming no right of requisition. The terms parallel the Edomite request (Deuteronomy 2:6). Sihon's refusal of commercially fair and politically non-threatening terms removes all ambiguity about the justice of the subsequent military response: the refuser of peace invites the war that follows.
29: 'Just as the sons of Esau who live in Seir and the Moabites who live in Ar did for me' — appeal to diplomatic precedent: Edom and Moab (whether by actual transaction or by Israel's passage through their territory without incident) are cited as nations that permitted the transit. The use of precedent in diplomatic negotiation is standard ANE practice; demonstrating that comparable transit has been safely granted by similar parties removes Sihon's potential objection that granting passage sets a dangerous precedent.
30: 'YHWH your God hardened his spirit and made his heart obstinate, so that he might give him into your hand' — the hardening formula (cf. Exodus 4:21; Deuteronomy 7:3; Deuteronomy 9:12: YHWH hardening Pharaoh). The theological claim is that YHWH's sovereignty operates through Sihon's own choice without removing his moral responsibility. Sihon's refusal was genuinely his own decision; YHWH used that decision to bring about the judgment against Sihon that the campaign required. The same divine-human double causation pattern governs the Pharaoh cycle in Exodus.
31: 'See, I have begun to give Sihon and his land before you. Begin to take possession' — the divine military commissioning at the campaign's opening. The verb hāḥēl (I have begun) marks divine initiative preceding human action: YHWH's giving is already in process; Israel's military action completes what YHWH has begun. In ANE royal victory inscriptions, the patron deity's prior grant is always the theological basis that the king's military success makes visible and historical.
32: 'Then Sihon came out against us, he and all his people, for battle at Jahaz' — Jahaz appears in the Mesha Stele (Moabite Stone, ca. 840 BCE) as a battlefield where Israel had fought against Moab, confirming the historical currency of this site in both Israelite and Moabite memory. The Mesha Stone's reference to Jahaz demonstrates that the battle traditions of this region persisted in regional consciousness across centuries and were recorded from multiple national perspectives.
33: 'YHWH our God delivered him before us, and we defeated him and his sons and all his people' — the divine passive of warfare: the victory is YHWH's gift first, Israel's military achievement second. This two-tier causation (divine gift + human military action) is the consistent pattern of Deuteronomy's holy war theology. The king's military success in ANE royal inscriptions always credits the patron deity first; Deuteronomy democratizes this theology to the whole covenant community rather than the royal figure.
34: 'We captured all his cities at that time and devoted to destruction every city — men, women, and children. We left no survivor' — the ḥērem (devotion/ban) applied to Sihon's cities. The concept of total consecration of a defeated enemy to the patron deity was known in ANE warfare: the Moabite Stone records Mesha devoting captive Israelites to his god Ashtar-Chemosh using the cognate term ḥrm. The ḥērem is the religious dimension of conquest: the defeated belong to YHWH, not to the conquerors as spoil or slaves.
35: 'Only the livestock we took as plunder for ourselves, and the spoil of the cities' — the standard exception to the ḥērem: persons are devoted, livestock and goods may be taken. This follows the ḥērem protocol (Numbers 31:9-12 provides a fuller version). Achan's violation of this protocol in Joshua 7 — taking gold and garments that belonged to YHWH — is the paradigm case of what the Sihon campaign successfully avoided. The precision of the permitted/prohibited categories is the covenantal war's legal structure.
36: 'From Aroer on the edge of the valley of the Arnon, and from the city that is in the valley, as far as Gilead, there was not a city too high for us' — the survey of the conquered territory. Aroer on the Arnon (modern Khirbet Arair) was the southernmost point of the Sihon kingdom; Gilead the northern highlands. The rhetorical echo is significant: the cities the spies once feared ('great and fortified up to heaven', Deuteronomy 1:28) proved conquerable — 'not a city too high for us.' YHWH's commission, once refused, is now vindicated.
37: 'Only to the land of the sons of Ammon you did not draw near, that is, all along the bank of the river Jabbok and the cities of the hill country' — the Jabbok (modern Zarqa river) was the boundary between Sihon's territory and Ammon. Israel's strict observance of the Jabbok boundary — not crossing into Ammon despite the military momentum of the Sihon campaign — demonstrates covenant integrity: the command regarding Ammon (Deuteronomy 2:19) is kept even when Sihon's defeat has created an opportunity to press further. Obedience to divine territorial limits proves the holiness-of-war principle is genuine, not merely rhetorical.
